mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 4 Series 425d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 425d engine (N47S1 or B47D20B) typically costs £2,000–£4,200 supply only depending on engine generation. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 4 Series 425d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine generation your 425d has. The N47S1 (pre‑LCI, 2014–2017) is a punchy twin‑turbo diesel but has a well‑documented weakness: timing chain failure. The B47D20B (LCI, 2017–2020) is the successor engine that addressed these timing chain issues and offers 224 bhp. Both engines demand strict maintenance – regular oil changes (8,000–10,000 miles) are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 4 Series 425d?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the pre‑LCI N47S1, the most serious issue is timing chain failure – the chain, guides, and tensioners can wear prematurely, causing chain stretch and potential catastrophic engine damage. On both N47S1 and B47D20B, common problems include EGR valve/cooler failure, DPF blockage from urban driving, and oil leaks (valve cover, oil filter housing).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a 425d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £5,000–£15,000 4 Series, a £2,000–£4,200 recon is 15–45% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped on an N47S1 and caused valve damage, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my 4 Series's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The pre‑LCI F32/F33/F36 (2014–2017) uses the N47S1 (N47D20T1) – check for the rear‑mounted timing chain design. The LCI (2017–2020) uses the B47D20B – the improved successor engine. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a 425d engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – timing chain wear (if caught early on N47S1), EGR valve failure, DPF blockage, oil leaks – are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped on an N47S1, the engine is an interference design – pistons will strike valves. In this case, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

problems.title: Timing Chain Failure — N47S1 (Pre‑LCI, 2014–2017)
problems.symptoms: Cold‑start rattle lasting 1‑3 seconds, metallic ticking from the engine, rattling persisting when warm, engine warning light, loss of power — catastrophic failure can occur without warning.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles (rear‑mounted chain design is labour‑intensive to access).
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (timing chain, tensioner, and guide replacement — engine or gearbox removal required).
problems.replacementCost: £2,000–£4,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The N47 timing chain is the defining weakness of this engine generation. The chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, against the firewall, making access labour‑intensive. The chain, guides, and tensioners can wear prematurely, causing chain stretch. If the chain stretches too far, it can "jump" a tooth or snap, causing pistons to strike valves in this interference engine. A £1,200–£2,500 preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a £2,000–£4,200 engine replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately. The B47 (2017‑onwards) addressed these issues with an improved timing chain design.

problems.title: EGR Valve / Cooler Failure (N47S1 & B47D20B)
problems.symptoms: Reduced power, rough idle, engine warning light, increased fuel consumption, black smoke from exhaust, failed emissions test.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if engine warning light appears.

problems.recommendation: EGR system failure is a common issue on both N47 and B47 diesel engines. The EGR valve can become clogged with soot, and the EGR cooler can develop leaks, leading to coolant loss. Some N47 engines were subject to a recall for EGR cooler leaks. Regular motorway driving and using high‑quality diesel can help reduce EGR clogging. If the engine warning light appears with EGR‑related codes, have the system diagnosed promptly.

problems.title: DPF Blockage (Urban Driving)
problems.symptoms: Reduced power, increased fuel consumption, engine warning light, DPF warning message, failed emissions test, oil level rising (fuel dilution).

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if DPF warning appears.

problems.recommendation: The 425d's twin‑turbo diesel is sensitive to driving patterns. If used primarily for short urban trips, the Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) will clog. Regular motorway runs (30+ minutes at 60+ mph) are essential to maintain the exhaust filtration system. Oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles are recommended rather than BMW's suggested longer intervals to prolong engine and DPF life.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Valve Cover & Oil Filter Housing (N47S1 & B47D20B)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the top or front of the engine.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 [PLACEHOLDER] (valve cover gasket or oil filter housing gasket replacement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: Both N47 and B47 engines are known for oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing gasket. If the oil filter housing gasket fails, oil can leak onto the serpentine belt, causing it to slip or break — which can then be ingested into the engine through the front crankshaft seal, causing catastrophic failure. If you notice oil spots under your 425d or a burning oil smell, investigate immediately. A £300–£800 gasket replacement is far cheaper than the consequences of a snapped belt.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW 4 Series 425d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is almost certain
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This BMW 4 Series 425d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* Pre‑LCI (N47S1): Timing chain inspected and any work documented
* LCI (B47D20B): Evidence of regular motorway driving (DPF‑friendly usage)
* A well‑maintained example with lower mileage
avoidIf: * Pre‑LCI (N47S1): Any sign of cold‑start rattle — this is the defining weakness
* No evidence of regular oil changes — both engines are intolerant of neglect
* DPF warnings on the dashboard — indicates urban‑only driving
* Poor service history — these engines demand strict maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 425d offers punchy twin‑turbo diesel performance in a stylish coupe, convertible, or Gran Coupe body. The pre‑LCI N47S1 is a capable engine with a well‑documented Achilles heel: timing chain failure. The LCI B47D20B addresses this with an improved design and delivers 224 bhp. Buy on service history above all else — a vehicle with documented timing chain maintenance and regular oil changes is a safe bet; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare 4 Series 425d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 4 Series 425d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any rattle for 1‑3 seconds? (timing chain warning on N47S1 — stop immediately)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (8,000–10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Timing Chain History — N47S1: Has the chain been inspected or replaced?
4. DPF Health — Has the vehicle done regular longer runs? (short trips kill DPFs)
5. EGR System — Any engine warning lights? Any EGR‑related codes?
6. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the valve cover and oil filter housing
7. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
